title: Near-Earth asteroids discovered over time
subtitle: Cumulative number of discovered asteroids with an orbit that brings them into proximity with Earth. This is broken down by size of the asteroid.
caption:
Near-Earth asteroids discovered over time

Cumulative number of discovered asteroids with an orbit that brings them into proximity with Earth. This is broken down by size of the asteroid.

Between 1990 and 2025, the cumulative count of near-Earth asteroids with orbits bringing them into proximity with Earth rises from near zero to approximately 40,000, broken down by size into three stacked bands. The vertical axis measures the cumulative number of discoveries from 0 to 40,000, while the horizontal axis spans years from 1990 to 2025. The largest band, colored orange and labeled "Smaller than 140 meters," forms the top layer and accounts for the majority of the total, reaching roughly 28,000 by 2025. The middle band, colored purple and labeled "Between 140 meters and 1 kilometer," sits beneath the orange layer and reaches approximately 11,000 by the end of the period. The bottom band, colored black and labeled "Larger than 1 kilometer," remains the smallest and flattest layer, showing very slow growth to just over 1,000 by 2025. The curve shows a distinct acceleration starting around 2005, where the slope of the orange and purple bands steepens noticeably compared to the flatter trajectory of the preceding decade. By 2010, the total count passes 7,000, and by 2020, the total exceeds 25,000. The black band representing the largest asteroids shows a steady but gradual increase throughout the entire thirty-five-year period, contrasting with the rapid expansion seen in the smaller size categories. The data shows that the vast majority of the growth in the cumulative total comes from the discovery of asteroids smaller than 140 meters.
